description: This figure presents the effect of aluminum content (3–10 wt.%) on the mechanical properties of Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steels after water quenching from 1150°C and aging at 550°C for 16 h. The chart comprises three stacked subplots: (1) upper panel shows mechanical strength versus Al content, plotting ultimate tensile strength (UTS, filled triangles) and 0.2% yield strength (YS, open triangles) for two carbon levels—0.85% C (dashed lines) and 0.95% C (solid lines); (2) middle panel displays ductility indicators including reduction of area (ψ, open circles) and elongation (δ, filled circles) versus Al content; (3) lower panel illustrates U-notch impact toughness (K_CU, diamonds) in J/cm² versus Al content. Key trends reveal that both UTS and YS remain relatively constant below ~7% Al but increase sharply thereafter, with higher carbon content consistently producing greater strength. Conversely, ductility (ψ and δ) gradually decreases with increasing Al. Impact toughness peaks at approximately 6–7% Al (~180 J/cm² for 0.85% C and ~160 J/cm² for 0.95% C) before declining significantly at higher Al contents. These data demonstrate the trade-off between strength and toughness, indicating that excessive Al (>7%) promotes precipitation hardening (likely κ-phase) that enhances strength but severely compromises impact resistance and ductility.

This optical micrograph displays the microstructure of an Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steel containing 4.6 wt.% aluminum, subjected to aging at 600°C for 16 hours. The image reveals equiaxed austenitic grains with distinct grain boundaries, along which the k-phase (carbide) has precipitated in a continuous network morphology. Additionally, fine intragranular precipitates are visible within the matrix. According to the caption and citation context, this intergranular carbide precipitation represents an embrittling microstructural feature that is highly detrimental to impact toughness. The micrograph illustrates the deleterious effect of low aluminum content (below the critical concentration) on microstructural stability, where carbon content drives carbide precipitation at grain boundaries rather than homogeneous distribution. This figure supports the paper's core argument for defining composition limits to avoid embrittling phases in FeMnAlC structural steels, specifically demonstrating the undesirable precipitation behavior that must be prevented through proper alloy design.

This figure presents two optical micrographs showing the microstructure of Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steel containing 10.5 wt.% Al, specifically illustrating the morphology and distribution of δ-ferrite (d-ferrite). Subfigure (a) displays the as-cast microstructure where δ-ferrite appears as a dark, continuous network or dendritic phase distributed within the lighter austenite matrix. Subfigure (b) shows the microstructure after hot deformation, where δ-ferrite has transformed into a chain-like morphology aligned along the elongated grain boundaries, with the austenite grains exhibiting significant deformation features. Both images share a scale bar of 30 μm. The micrographs demonstrate that when aluminum content exceeds 10%, δ-ferrite forms as an embrittling phase that reduces technological plasticity and enhances mechanical anisotropy, while hot working partially modifies the ferrite distribution.

Optical micrographs comparing the microstructural evolution of Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steels as a function of aluminum content after granulation and pressing at 1150°C followed by quenching from 1100°C. Subfigure (a) shows the microstructure of steel containing 9.8% Al, exhibiting a homogeneous austenitic matrix with equiaxed grains and absence of δ-ferrite. Subfigure (b) displays the microstructure of steel with 14% Al, revealing the presence of δ-ferrite (labeled as 'δ' with an arrow indicating the phase) as a secondary phase formed along grain boundaries or within the matrix. The accompanying citation states that δ-ferrite reaches approximately 10% volume fraction at 14% Al, whereas rapid solidification (jet spraying) can suppress δ-ferrite formation up to 12% Al. These images demonstrate the critical role of aluminum content in promoting embrittling δ-ferrite formation, supporting the definition of composition limits (Al < 9.7% for optimum microstructure) for structural applications requiring UTS ≥ 1100 MPa and impact toughness > 50 J/cm². Both images share a 30 μm scale bar.

This figure displays saturation magnetization curves (4πJs, in Gauss) versus carbon content (0.8–1.06 wt%) for Fe-30Mn-9Al-C austenitic steels subjected to various aging treatments. The graph contains multiple curves representing aging temperatures ranging from 350°C to 550°C with durations of 16 hours (dashed lines) and 100 hours (solid lines). The data reveals that saturation magnetization increases with carbon content across all conditions, with maximum values reaching approximately 2200–2400 G at 450–500°C for 100 hours, indicating extensive precipitation of magnetic κ-phase particles. In contrast, aging at lower temperatures (350–400°C) yields significantly lower magnetization values (<1000 G), suggesting limited κ-phase formation. The caption states that carbon is the most diffusion-mobile element and serves as the leading component at the onset of aging. These magnetic measurements provide qualitative information on the intensity of the austenite aging process and the kinetics of κ-phase precipitation in Fe-Mn-Al-C alloys.

This figure presents a comprehensive three-panel plot illustrating the effect of carbon content (0.3–1.1 wt.%) on the mechanical properties of Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steels with fixed compositions of 9 wt.% Al and 30 wt.% Mn. The top panel displays mechanical strength parameters including ultimate tensile strength (UTS, filled triangles) and 0.2% offset yield strength (0.2%YS, open triangles) in MPa. The middle panel shows ductility metrics: reduction of area (ψ, open circles) and elongation (δ, filled circles) in percentage. The bottom panel presents U-notch impact toughness (Kcu, diamonds) in J/cm². Two heat treatment conditions are compared: water quenching from 1050°C (dashed curves) versus water quenching followed by aging at 550°C for 16 hours (solid curves). The data reveal that both UTS and yield strength increase monotonically with carbon content due to solid-solution hardening. Conversely, ductility and impact toughness exhibit optimal values around 0.7–0.8 wt.% C, peaking at approximately 50% reduction of area and 120 J/cm² impact toughness, respectively, before declining sharply at higher carbon concentrations due to grain boundary carbide precipitation during aging, or at lower carbon levels (<0.7%) due to the presence of δ-ferrite.

This figure presents a comprehensive mechanical property assessment of Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steels, specifically illustrating the effect of manganese content (ranging from 24 to 34 wt%) on tensile strength, ductility, and impact toughness. The data comprises three vertically stacked subplots sharing a common x-axis (Mn content in wt%): the upper subplot displays ultimate tensile strength (UTS, solid triangles) and 0.2% yield strength (YS, open triangles) in MPa, showing both strengths peak around 26-30 wt% Mn (~1200 MPa for UTS and ~1000 MPa for YS) before declining sharply beyond 32 wt% Mn; the middle subplot tracks ductility parameters including reduction of area (ψ, open circles) and elongation (δ, solid circles) in percent, revealing a plateau region between 28-32 wt% Mn followed by catastrophic deterioration at 34 wt% Mn; and the lower subplot shows U-notch impact toughness (Kcu, open diamonds) in J/cm², maintaining relatively stable values (~100-115 J/cm²) from 24-32 wt% Mn before plummeting to approximately 35 J/cm² at 34 wt% Mn. The caption specifies the alloy composition as Fe-9Al-0.9C with varying Mn, subjected to water quenching from 1050°C and aging at 550°C for 16 hours. The citation context explains that Mn increases the solubility of Al and C in the γ-solid solution, thereby reducing κ-phase precipitation during aging. The figure demonstrates that optimal structural properties—balancing high strength (UTS ≥ 1100 MPa) with acceptable toughness (>50 J/cm²)—are achieved within a narrow Mn window of approximately 28.5-30.5 wt%, with embrittlement occurring at higher Mn concentrations due to microstructural degradation.
